ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for uncoated biscuit sticks; closest match is the general biscuit food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers biscuits and crackers broadly; no specific sub-code for uncoated biscuit sticks in the standard hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for uncoated biscuit sticks; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion of uncoated biscuit sticks is approximately 25 g (roughly 10–12 sticks), providing about 112 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Biscuit stick, uncoated (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Biscuit stick, uncoated (any flavour)' (record ID F11080005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for uncoated biscuit sticks in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 74 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain water crackers / refined-flour crackers (GI ~72–78, mean ~74) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Refined wheat flour base with zero fibre supports a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (74 × 63.12 × 0.25) / 100 = 11.7 g per 25 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 398 mg/100 g with negligible potassium and zero dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: At 398 mg sodium per 100 g and with no compensatory potassium or fibre, even a 25 g portion contributes ~100 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit intake and choose lower-sodium snack alternatives such as unsalted nuts or plain rice crackers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (63.1 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, yielding a high GI of ~74
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of 63.1 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g, zero dietary fibre, and an estimated GI of 74 produces a high glycaemic load that will cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; people with type 2 diabetes should avoid this food and substitute high-fibre crackers (≥3 g fibre/100 g) or vegetable sticks.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~8 g/100 g from palm oil, trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, and sodium 398 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 8 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat per 100 g from palm-oil processing, together with near-threshold sodium, makes this snack unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and choose snacks with unsaturated fats and lower sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g and saturated fat ~8 g/100 g from palm oil with no anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Trans fat is a hard gate for rheumatoid arthritis suitability; the estimated 0.2 g trans fat per 100 g from palm-oil processing, combined with pro-inflammatory saturated fat and zero fibre, makes this food unsuitable for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Uncoated biscuit sticks are slender, baked snack items made primarily from refined wheat flour, vegetable oil (typically palm oil), salt, and leavening agents. They are produced in various flavours — including plain, sesame, and savoury seasonings — but share the same base nutritional profile regardless of flavour. The texture is crisp and dry, similar to a thin breadstick or cracker, with a mild savoury taste. They are widely sold in Singapore and across Asia as a convenience snack, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a between-meal snack. Nutritionally, they are energy-dense at 449 kcal per 100 g, with a high refined carbohydrate content of 63.1 g/100 g, moderate fat at 18.8 g/100 g (predominantly from palm oil, contributing approximately 8 g saturated fat and an estimated 0.2 g trans fat per 100 g), and moderate protein at 6.8 g/100 g. Dietary fibre is absent (0 g/100 g), and sugar is negligible (0 g/100 g). Sodium is near the upper borderline at 398 mg/100 g. The absence of fibre combined with a refined flour base gives these sticks an estimated glycaemic index of approximately 74 (high), making them unsuitable for blood glucose management. They are not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The food is classified as ultra-processed and contains gluten; it is generally considered vegetarian, vegan, and halal-compatible in its plain form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in biscuit sticks?
Are biscuit sticks high in sodium?
What is the GI of biscuit sticks?
Are biscuit sticks suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F11080005) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from known palm-oil composition profiles for similar biscuit products; these secondary values are marked accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain refined-flour crackers as the reference food.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a savoury biscuit product with no added sugar by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — refined wheat flour base with no added fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the original database record. saturated_fat_g (~8.0 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (~0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from palm-oil composition data for similar biscuit products, as the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions; these are approximate values. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this generic biscuit stick category are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this record. GI of 74 is an analogous estimate based on plain refined-flour crackers/water crackers from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for uncoated biscuit sticks was found in approved sources. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' present in the input cultural data has been removed as it is inconsistent with the refined wheat flour composition confirmed by the HPB source. This food is not cuisine-specific (generic international snack product); therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules.
